sentences.text: Good afternoon--
Good afternoon.
Yeah.
My name is Kurt, K-U-R-T, Saunders, S-A-U-N-D-E-R-S.
My name at birth was Kurt also, and it was Schatzker, S-C-H-A-T-Z-K-E-R.
No, I don't have any other names, except that my legal American name is Kent, K-E-N-T.
[HEBREW]
Not 'til I came to America.
An [INAUDIBLE] man called me here Kurtle Turtle.
[LAUGHS]
I was born on June 11, 1919 in Vienna.
I am now 77 years old.
Do you want me to spell it the German way?
In German, it's spelled W-I-E-N.
In English, it's spelled V like Victor, I-E-N-N-A.
Austria.
In German, it's Osterreich.
OK.
No.
My parents, both of them were born in what's Poland today, which was Galicia.
In America, they call them Galic, right, Galicianas.
That's where they were born.
But they moved to Vienna, when, in my father's case, his mother died at a young age and he was left behind.
And his father, who was a rabbi, moved to Vienna.
And then my father, at a young age-- he was 11 years-- followed to Vienna.
My mother had a very similar story.
Her mother died.
Her father remarried and left his two children, a son and a daughter, in Vienna with a brother who had-- didn't have any children.
And he came to America.
That was in 1904.
Then he brought his family, the children of the second marriage, to America.
So I have mishpacha here.
Not my father's side, but on my mother's side I have mishpacha here.
And what did your father do in Vienna?
What was his occupation?
After the First World War, he started off-- things were kind of rough in Austria.
They'd lost the war.
He started off as a custom peddler and exchanged piece goods, yard goods, for food, and then established himself-- got a store in the countryside.
Before the war, he served seven years in the Austrian Army.
And at the age of 11, when he came to Vienna, he was peddling picture postcards door to door.
Then he got a job where he worked in a liquor-- actually, it was not a liquor store.
It was a store at-- at a market.
It opened up 5 o'clock in the morning and people came in to have a shot of liquor.
He helped out in that market.
And then at night, he worked for the people that owned the liquor store.
He shined their shoes.
He cleaned their house.
He was a sleep-in maid and he couldn't go to sleep 'til everybody had gone to bed and they put a board across the bath tub and a mattress on top of it and that's where he went to sleep.
I grew up partially in Vienna and partially in Mitterdorf.
Yes.
That's where the store was.
And that is known as Mitterdorf Murztal.
It's on the way-- it's in the province of Styria.
It's on the way to Graz.
The railroad comes from Vienna across the Semmering.
Anybody who knows Austria knows that pass.
And--
The state is Styria, Steiermark.
The Murztal is the-- 'tal' means-- Murztal is--
No.
No.
The Murztal is a river.
It's the valley of the Murz.
There's mountains on each side.
The house was beautiful.
The store was in the front of the house.
And in the back, we had an apartment.
We also had an apartment at the second floor, which was used by the family that came to visit us, relatives.
In summer time, they came out to the country.
It was countryside.
And in the back, eventually they had-- it was built during the Depression years.
We had a house that was a rental property for four families, plus a caretaker, five.
Yes, we owned both of them.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, here's how it happened.
At first, when my father was a custom peddler, he used to commute to the different towns.
And then when he finally settled there, there were-- he only came home once a week to get merchandise and so on.
And after a while realized-- my mother realized there were double expenses, a household in Vienna, a household in the country, and decided to move out to the country.
And at that time, I was six years old.
My younger brother was three years old.
We moved out there.
I started school.
And there was an incident later on, a anti-Semitic incident.

On the way home from school, I was jumped with five other boys, came home with a black eye and told my father who had been First Sergeant in the Army seven years now-- I told my father what transpired.
And his answer was, it's impossible.
Five cannot beat you up at the same time, only two at the most, maybe three.
And in the future, you go to the-- for the biggest guy, draw blood.
Hit him in the nose.
Poke him in the eyes.
Draw blood, because they're all a bunch of cowards, and then run.
This was the first time I was ever beaten and it was it last time.
In the future, I beat them.
I listened to my dad and it worked.
At that time, I was almost seven years old when this happened.
Now, my father--
Well, this-- this incident, my father went to school to complain to the teacher.
And the teacher took it all down and asked him, what did they do?
They called you a dirty Jew.
Actually, the word in Germany and Austria really, they never called you dirty Jew.
They called you a [GERMAN], which means a pig Jew.
Sow is a pig.
[GERMAN]
Yes.
OK?
That was the common word.
That's how they called the Jews.
Anyway, he went to the teacher to complain about it.
And the teacher got the names of the five boys.
And he said he'll take care of it.
And as soon as my father left, he called these five boys out and asked them how come they attacked me and tried to beat me up.
And the answer was that I have called them dirty Christians.
So he got his whip out and I got it.
And that's when our parents decided I should go to school in Vienna, that this was not the place for me.
My brother, on the other hand, was not-- didn't get into any problem.
He wouldn't fight back.
He only waited for me to come home during vacation time.
That's when he got even with these boys.
And I became involved in fights constantly, every time I came home.
Normally, my brother would sit on the school bus in the back of-- in the front near the driver.
When I came home, he sat in the back.
And when I went to pick him up, I had fights on the bus.
I had fights on the ski slopes.
And over the years, my parents were afraid that someday they're going to kill me.
But they didn't.
We were the only family, the two children, two brothers.
On the same street we had a Jewish man living with a Christian woman.
She was a divorcee.
And on Tuesdays, the dentist, the single dentist, single man who was a dentist, used to drive in from Vienna, had a practice there.
And he would leave Thursday afternoon and go back to Vienna.
Those were the Jews.
Yes.
Let me-- yes and no.
We knew they were anti-Semites.
Like this teacher, we knew that he belonged to the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN] group there.
And we knew that he was a Fascist.
We knew about others that were hiding it, but you could tell after a while, after the Nazi party had been outlawed, who the Nazis were.
But our experience with the customers, we were not in the main-- where the store was, it was not in the main place, plaza, it's-- where the other stores were.
This was on a side street.
And the customers that came to us, very friendly business.
My father ran a credit business and never charged any interest.

As a matter of fact, the people used to buy merchandise would by Christmas time-- the big shopping was twice a year, Christmas time, then they'll pay at Easter for the merchandise they bought at Christmas.
They buy Easter merchandise, clothing, and they'll pay at Christmas time.
Or they pay it in installments.
After all-- after a while, this became the Depression years, 1930s and so on.
So on the whole, we didn't have any problem.
Even after the Nazis came-- well, that's another story.
They came in and it became critical, because they posted a Nazi stormtrooper in front of it and-- while I was in Vienna.
And I came home in between the few days from the time that they marched in, in those few days, they had come into the house, had pushed my father down into the basement with a rifle and bayonet.
They forced him-- we had just received a big shipment of coal.
They said, you must be hiding weapons under the coal.
The forced him to shovel the coal from one side to the other side and shovel it back.
It was-- it was more fear.
No, I was not.
I was in Vienna.
When I came home, that is what I was told about what transpired.
They took the keys.
They took the money.
They took the bank books.
And they post a man.

And they put-- they wrote on the sidewalk in front of the store, actually in the street, they put down 'Jew,' 'Jude.' And after a couple of days, the chief of police, who happened to be a Nazi too, as it turned out-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- came and apologized, returned the keys, returned the money, returned everything.
They removed the guard, but they still left the 'Jew' written on the sidewalk.
And needless to say, people were afraid to come in.
But at nighttime, after dark, people came to the rear door.
Some brought money.
Some brought food.
They tried to help.
Yes.
In-- when I was a youngster, my mother had an aunt in Vienna.
I spent a few month with her.
And then I got a foster parent.
And I had constant changes.
Then I was in a school, boarding school.
And another aunt of mine came in.
My father's sister came in.
Her husband had been arrested.
He was a Communist.
And I didn't-- actually, if I tell you the story why she was living in my father's apartment, because her own mother wouldn't put up with her.
Relatives.
Yeah.
And strangers too.
No, I-- yes.
Well, there were Jews in Vienna.
Yeah.
But the area that you were in--
Roughly 10% of population of Vienna was Jewish.
I had one wonderful thing, my cousin, who is alive today and is in Israel.
There's a unique thing.
My cousin was like a brother to me.
And to his mother, I was like another son.
He-- there was a four months age difference.
He was four months older than me.
And my mother couldn't nurse me.
His mother nursed me.
So there was a very close relationship there.
No.
No.
In Vienna?
Well, nothing like out in the countryside.
It was constant.
When I came home, there was always some remark, like my brother-- my father's Jewish name, Hebrew name was Pasach.
So even though my brother's name was Walter, he was called by some of them Little Pasach.
And they were always needling, you know.
We knew it was there.
In Vienna, naturally, with a much larger Jewish population-- and I went to Hebrew school.
And we belonged to the temple and my uncle was the president of the temple.
Another uncle was on the board of the temple.
And holidays were observed.
My parents would come in on the high holidays, would come in for Passover to Vienna.
So we knew we had a Jewish home.
My grandfather was a rabbi, so you couldn't miss it.
And I belonged to Jewish clubs, like the Hakoah, which is a sports club.
I was on a swimming team.
I was on a wrestling team.
So I was brought up as a Jew.
We knew, even though there were times that, you know, you're a young fella.
Your mind works and you say to yourself, maybe the Jewish solution would be if all Jews were to convert.
Did that-- did that cross your mind?
Yes.
About the age I was 14, 15 years old.

And when you hear history, that even though the Jews in Spain converted it didn't make any difference, because 500 years later they're still called [SPANISH] and they're still called-- their Jewish background.
And then you realize that even in Vienna, there are many converts that have married Gentiles and mixed marriages.
And especially after Hitler came, there were kids that were anti-Semitic in school and discovered all of a sudden they had a Jewish ancestor.
Now, to us, it was easy.
We were Jews.
To them, that was horrible.
Some committed suicide.
Yes, I do.
It depend how much money you had.
Yes.
Mine was on Tisha B'Av, at the [INAUDIBLE] temple in the 20th District.
Temple was full of people, over 1,000 people.
And I had to perform well, because one uncle was the president.
The other uncle was on the board.
And it would be a [INAUDIBLE], right, if I failed the bar mitzvah.
And then after the bar mitzvah, we had a little party at the-- another uncle, my grandfather's brother, who had raised my mother and her brother.
He had a cafe.
We didn't-- it was in the evening, after sundown.
Yes.
Well, this was before Hitler.
It was 1932, my bar mitzvah.
In-- when they marched in.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Well, German troops moved very fast into Austria.
And they moved for the borders, to prevent anybody from escaping.
They occupied the borders with Italy, with Czechoslovakia, with Yugoslavia, with Hungary.
They occupied all the borders to Switzerland, to Luxembourg.
The only border they didn't occupy was the border between Germany and Austria, because Austria became part of it.
And I remember being in Vienna when Hitler got into Vienna.
He was up at the Hotel Bristol on the balcony making a speech.
And the women went hysterical.
They fell on the ground.
They kissed the ground and you would think it was Elvis Presley out there, you know.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Yes.
I was there.
And there were-- and Hitler said-- I remember even the words he said, which were outstanding, that if not-- God is with him, because if not for God, he wouldn't be here in Austria.
God is with him.
And naturally, the weeks before the Nazis marched into Austria-- I don't know whether you're aware of it-- the Nazi party had been outlawed for years.
But in 1938, it became legal again.
And there was a fellow by the name of Schusschnigg who was the premier.
And he invited them to come in.
Actually, he and another fellow by the name of Seyss-Inquart, who had been put into the government.
See , the story was in the newspapers.
Now that we have the National Socialist in the Austrian government, there's nothing to fear.
We have the horses before the wagon.
But the horses ran away with the wagon.
You can't say increase, because it was always there.
No, not really.
Not really.
Yes.
How could you miss it?
We had Jewish refugees, German refugees come through, walking, hiking through Austria on the way to Palestine.
And my mother always had food waiting for them, not only them, every beggar.
My father used to tease my mother that she must have a mark on the fence, because all the beggars came.
Even during the Depression years, my mother always had bread and had big pot of soup on the-- on the stove to feed them.
And now the refugees came, the German refugees.
Oh, yes.
At least I believed them.
Well, I had something else.
I don't know whether I mentioned to you I read a lot.
And I had-- might sound funny too.
I had books that I read on the toilet.
When I'm sitting on the toilet, I had books that I read.
I still have that habit.
And there were two books that I really went through.
One was Hitler's And I believed what he said that he is going to do.
And the other one was Karl Marx's
And when Hitler came into Austria, I believed that he's going to do it, even though my uncle, his answer was, there are Jews living in Germany, and they are OK.
I'm not a Communist.
I have never been accused of stealing or doing anything dishonest.
Why should I worry?
That was my mother's brother.
And my-- we had a different thing.
We didn't expect-- my parents didn't expect anything to happen, except they knew that they had no-- no more livelihood.
They could not exist in a town where they're the only Jews and the street, they could be blocked off easily.
And they kept the customers out of the store.
But I know what we did is when I came home, I helped shipping back the merchandise.
We knew we were out of business.
He couldn't pay his bills.
So for days and days, we packed and shipped it back to the manufacturers in Vienna.
Even to the last minute, we wanted to be-- do honest business.
We started off first.

I was the one that after I'd come home, my father sent me back to Vienna to see whether there was any way of getting out of Austria, because we were hoping to get affidavits from America, from my father-- my mother's family, our grandfather.
But we also felt there wasn't any future, because the livelihood was gone.
My uncle's business was different in Vienna.
They did tremendous amount of business when the Nazis came in, because--
Men's clothing and ladies clothing.
They were buying-- in Nazi Germany, they had shortages already.
And here in Vienna, they had everything.
They came in and they bought, bought.
But then within a few weeks, my uncle had a commissar put into his store.
The guy controlled-- he used to be a tailor working for him.
The guy became the boss now, controlled the business.
Eventually they did leave.
They did leave.
But--
We were the first one to plan it.
Yes.
Just plain Nazis.
The stormtroopers.
Well, the funny thing is that most of the offensive things that were done, really offensive to Jews, were not done by the German Army that came in.
It was done by Austrian Nazis, some in uniform, some not in uniform.
They-- first of all, you had to have an Aryan pass to have a swastika, have the Hakenkrusz the Germans call it, to wear the pin.
If you wore it, you had to-- again, there were spot checks.
You had to show your Aryan papers.
They issued papers that you're an Aryan.
So people were rounded up in the streets.
People were taken out of the houses.
They were going from house to house, apartment houses.
These are not private homes.
They would see the superintendent and ask him, do you have any complaints about any of the Jews in your building?
And if he had complaints, they would get these people out on the street, beat them up.
Or it was winter time.

One of the things that they really loved to do and it happened to a cousin of ours, roll up your pants-- and some people wear long johns, roll up the long johns, then they spill lye on the sidewalk, now scrub the sidewalk.
And the lye would eat right into the skin, down to the flesh.
Or they beat them up.
Or they marched them, especially when Jews, religious Jews with the beards, that was a great joy to them to have them march, throw rotten eggs at them, spit on them, you know.
So--
Yes.
Yes.

The fear always was that they would come in those command cars, they called them, that the police used when they wanted to block off a street when there was a riot or something like that in pre-Hitler days.
Now the Nazis used that.
They would block of a whole street and round up Jewish people, like questioning them to identify them.
I was caught in one of those one day.
But I realized it was on the Taborstrasse in 2nd District.
I knew the area was [INAUDIBLE].
There was a-- I don't know what you call it.
It was actually a connection between Taborstrasse and next street, through an apartment building, that you could walk through and get out on the other side.
And that's the way I escaped.
I saw them pick people out of the lineups at the different embassies in Vienna.
The people were trying to get papers to leave Austria, go to different countries.
Like the American embassy, a lot of people were lined up at the embassy.
And they would pick out people, usually the elderly.
And I haven't seen it, but I was told that people who survived, they took them to the park.
And the Nazis would line up facing each other, a row of Nazis about 100 feet long.
And then you had to run between them, called [GERMAN].
Each one had a club.
They would hit you with it.
If you were younger you can run faster.
You only got hit by every third or fourth.
If you were older, you never made it to the end.
And I saw the trucks that they loaded up with the people that they picked in the streets.
No.
No.
No.
That happened to my father, the time with the coal.
No.
No.
I wasn't there.
They came into my parents' home, yes.
No.
This is the same time.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
No.
I-- when I came home to Mitterdorf, I had to-- my brother and I and my parents had to report to the police twice a week.
No, we just had to report.
Yes.
I don't know.
It's a very silly thing, because basically what are we going to do, dissolve the government, be revolutionaries against the Nazis?
And as a matter of fact, when we escaped, when he left, my father reported that we had gone to Vienna, that we were in Vienna now.
We didn't tell them that we were escaping to Belgium.
Not in Vienna, no.
OK.
